result:
All done — the spec is fully implemented and verified. Used 3 of the 10 build cycles.

**What I implemented** in `src/CsvRow.calr` (Calor source, compiled to C# by the project's Calor SDK), following the fixture's existing conventions (compact signatures, `§E{}` effect declarations, sequential `f00N` ids):

- **`EscapeField(str) -> str`** — calls the existing `NeedsQuoting`; when quoting is needed it returns the field wrapped in double-quotes with internal quotes doubled (via `replace`), otherwise returns the field verbatim.
- **`JoinRow(List<str>) -> str`** — escapes each field and joins with commas via a `§EACH` fold; the empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow(str) -> List<str>`** — an index-based scanner: a field starting with `"` is parsed to the next un-doubled quote (the raw slice is extracted with `substr` and un-doubled with one `replace`), other fields run to the next comma or end of line; a trailing comma produces a final empty field, and the empty line parses to a single empty field.

**Two notable findings along the way:**
- `§LIST` still requires an explicit `§/LIST{id}` closer (unlike structural tags, whose closers were removed) — the first build failed without it.
- The effect-enforcement pass maps `§PUSH` (list add) to the `mut` effect unconditionally, with no exemption for locally allocated collections, so `SplitRow` declares `§E{mut}`. This stays within the spec's purity constraint — no I/O, no global state; the mutation is confined to the result list the function itself allocates. The other functions keep `§E{}`.

**Verification:** `dotnet build` succeeds cleanly with effects enforcement on. I also compiled the generated C# into a scratch console project (outside the source tree, so no test files were added) and exercised it: 20 targeted cases covering every spec rule (empty field/line, trailing comma, embedded commas/quotes/CR/LF, lone-quote fields) plus round-trip `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields)) == fields` over 9 adversarial field lists — all pass.
